author: @AaronHarper
text:
Hiya Mate!  I like this series, but the trouble is that most preppers like the *idea* of preparedness, but when it comes time to actually deal with an issue they are the first to winge.  Here in the states, we have a lot of preppers, who claimed to be able to "survive anything", usually while brandishing their firearms.  Covid caused some of them to retreat to their bunkers, eat their two year food supply in a month, re-emerge 20 kilos heavier and complain about wearing a mask.  The point of this is that may self-styled preppers are not willing to be part of the solution... they just like the feeling of having done something about a perceived future threat, real or not.  Don't pay any mind to naysayers who are not interested in expending effort to solve their own problems.

The only thing I would recommend in addition is some study and work on reducing power consumption, boring as that may be.  PFC and all that is good for small gains in existing systems, but the Dynapod is about reinventing power generation from the ground up.  Lets examine how to work within the the relatively small power budget of the Dynapod to accomplish everyday things, particularly those things that will need done in an emergency, focusing on Maslow's first tier, then the second.  This probably won't be everyone's cup of tea, but I believe that it is just as important to do useful work within the power constraints of the Dynapod as it is to generate the power in the first place.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to be honest mate what struck me was not that prepares might make it - rather they might well buy it - to be honest I think this is an idea for a budding entrepreneur as my guess is there is money to be made here - that aside - I agree wholeheartedly with what you are saying - cheers

replies.author: @AaronHarper
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering agreed, though I find it disappointing that the average "prepared" person has done less than nothing in preparing themselves mentally, physically, or in terms of skills or knowledge.  Rather, they spend the money they do have on obvious preparation to rob their neighbors.

That said, their money spends well, and if such a thing can be marketed to those folks, those that spend money "prepping" and those who actually prepare, you might do remarkably well.

author: @judyofthewoods
text:
I'm totally with you there. I guess, as one of them mad preppers, I would be! I've got The Human Powered Home. Managed to find it second hand years ago. Also, years ago I picked an exercise bike out of a skip to make a dynapod and had a lucky opportunity to buy an ex army field telephone hand-crank 12V generator for which I made a stand and got a machine shop to form me a bar shaped like a square wave as a pedal crank. That said, the dynapod is still project 217/B on my project list, and the genny is packed away whilst my lights and laptop are powered by solar and mycro-hydro at my off-grid home. Come an EMP I probably won't be powering a laptop (if it survived). Tried that with the crank genny, and it's bl***y hard work. I definitely wouldn't make electricity for a 1000W blender! LEDs only from there on, maybe a radio, if I get on with that Faraday cage project 217/K.

What a lot of preppers don't take into consideration, or at least not in a basic way, is how to have all those mechanical things when the shtf comes in the form of an EMP. If they do, they go all complicated with generators etc. stored in Faraday cages, but a dynapod would give them all those things in a more robust way. Besides, when we get deeper into the grand solar minimum, Carrington type events may not be one-offs. These preppers would have to have a Faraday room or house to keep all the electrical gizzmos safe at all times.

Of course, you can power a lot of human powered tools directly. All hand tools are made that way, but legs are a lot stronger, and two do even more work. Rotational motion is also more efficient and ergonomic than reciprocal, e.g. for sawing, sanding, grating. And no need to wait for the s**t to hit the fan to get those benefits.

author: @dexterdixon2000
text: Enjoying the series but I pick up a couple of points from this video - A well matched electricity generator directly driven off pedals (no chain/gears) can be over 90% efficient. Secondly, including battery and BMS loss in the equation isn't fair because your direct mechanical option doesn't have storage. I imagine a directly driven generator powering a matched rpm motor for the devices would be similar (or better) than the chain/belt losses - and quieter (which is all loss). I think there is a lot to be said for direct mechanical drive but I don't think it's vastly more efficient than correctly matched generator+motor combinations.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @johnoddvar1967
text: Its make sense. Some of the great new equipment we have is nice, but we might live without a lot of it. I find your videos inspiering. I made a screwdriver in my work friday. My colege mencioned that it is cheaper to buy one. I agree, but it might be economic to know how to make one.  Playing around with stuff makes your mind think and often you can learn that things might be posible that you have been told not. I liked your grinder to, but i think it would have helped a litle whit a flywheel on thatone. Its of course cheaper to by new drilbits, but you can do a lot more whit the drilbits if you know how to grind them. You might grind them in different chapes or lenghts. If you chould dril holes in pipes it is good with a realy short one at least if its cabeles inside the pipe. If you shold drill in wood its faster if you change the angle from 120 to perhaps 90. And if the material is harder than normal 175 wil help you. Sometimes you can get a more acurate adjustment with a manual powred machine. I love your videos.

author: @huckmiddeke665
text:
Hey Robert,
I believe that the story of prepping to react is an old one.
To me diy low-tech energy  harvesting and muscle powered devices are not about "prepping to react", but "living to upgrade".
The new story of low footprints which is lived in ecovillages and so called developing countries is one of approaching resilience.
The benefits far outweighs the necessity. 
Greetings and gratitude from an offgrid lifestyle, 
Huck

replies.text: Here in NB Canada, that common sense way of things and doing, has not gone away. Probably because we're the only have not province in Canada. That said, it seems that those willing to mend, repurpose and create, will fair better in the long run.For they are super innovative.

author: @nonyabusiness9747
text: Thanks for breaking down everything so simply for us noobs.

replies.author: @nonyabusiness9747
replies.text: I often wonder how I can generate and store electricity.

replies.author: @nonyabusiness9747
replies.text: Dynopod?

replies.author: @nonyabusiness9747
replies.text: We live offgrid. Have a very basic solar system. Someday.. we will power a deep freeze and make ice.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am glad you like the vids mate there are lots of good methods I wouldn't use a dynapod - it sort of defeats the purpose - your own windmill maybe or if you have a stream water power?

author: @karlmyers6518
text: That comment did ruffle my feathers a little.  I learned a lot from this series.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you always get that kind of thing mate - but they tend to be one in a 100 - most folks do see the point

author: @geoffreylohff3876
text:
As I have watched this series, I think of my "neighbors"... the Amish.

They have lots of good ideas on electricity-free living.  👍

replies.author: @angelusmendez5084
replies.text:
Thanks for the idea, I'm searching books of mechanic devices and I haven't thought of the Amish.

In case you're interested, in a quick search I found this book on Amish devices, Living Without Electricity - Stephen Scott

replies.author: @angelusmendez5084
replies.text: Recently, on the NoTechMagazine, I read an article about the maker community in Cuba 😅

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am going to look up that book Angelus mentioned

replies.author: @judyofthewoods
replies.text: You can also get idea from Lehman's catalogue - on or off-line.

replies.author: @charlesrichards5389
replies.text: It's called "Hook a horse to it." 😉

author: @ColRubyDimples
text: I'd love to convert this old jacket holder into one of these! It's extra incentive to get on the thing! Thanks for all you do!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @tom8437
text: With the wide availability of battery operated power tools, I think the best thing for preppers would be an efficient pedal-powered battery charger. This would also appeal to many non-preppers. I get that it's not as efficient as direct pedal power. But it would be far more practical.

replies.author: @tom8437
replies.text: Actually, it doesn't have to be one or the other. The Dynapod can have many direct-drive attachments, and one of them can be a battery charger. Where do I sign up for that one?!

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: @tom8437 I was going to post, "it doesn't have to be one or the other" after reading your first comment, and then see you realized that minutes later. 😊  That's really the beauty of this.  As Rob referred to it in his last video, it's really like a swiss-army knife of a machine.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Like Cat - I read your second comment too - the multifunction would do that too!

replies.author: @judyofthewoods
replies.text: @tom8437 Indeed. There are now very impressive battery operated chainsaws. Try taking a dynapod directly to a tree on a slope. Horses for courses.

author: @raywhatsthisfor1283
text: I've always thought the hydrogen (power/electric)generators were a good idea. Think about it.. you wont always have fuel/petrol  for a generator  or may not propane or natural gas for an unlimited time!!! So a generator that works off water...abit like mrstanley meyer,????

replies.author: @ancapftw9113
replies.text: If you live in a wooded area, you could try a wood gas generator.  You can tie the gas directly into a gas generator.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is a good idea mate but you still have to get the hydrogen the. most popular method for home production is electrolysis - for which you need electricity - in industry it is steam cracking

replies.author: @raywhatsthisfor1283
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering yeah..that would be the most difficult part. I still wonder how mr. Meyer was able to get his dune buggy to run from southern California to the east coast or vise versa??? With water???  There is a video of the buggy water fill up and others explaining how his idea worked??? Its one of those things that would help this planet!!! But the people who know about it... will let this world die in our pollution!! Thanks again for your work, and videos!!

author: @michaeltucker8645
text: You turning into a pepper now

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: I believe Rob was born a "prepper"!  😄  It's definitely a natural instinct for him, the way he looks at resources.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - I just hate to throw away and buy mate - I much prefer to reuse

replies.author: @michaeltucker8645
replies.text: Same here. I'm still going thru all my throw away stuff make sure nothing can be reused, before I have a yard sale for the rest. Then I'll probably donate anything left from that. I am a prepper tho haha

author: @Peter_Schluss-Mit-Lustig
text:
Does the Rule of efficiency also apply for flywheel storage?

I mean a sufficiently large flywheel acts almost exactly like a capacitor for mechanical energy and if the losses to friction are mitigated it might make things a little more efficient

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it might well do mate

replies.author: @Roteberht35
replies.text: I'd imagine you can use an industrial spring as a kind of battery as well

text: Robert, I totally agree with you, that's the trouble with this world today we rely on to much Technology and a lot of people don't even have the basic skills to be able to survive in a life and death situation, most kids today would not know what to if the Internet went down, they would not have a clue how to use there hands to survive a situation, and to be truthfulbin this current climate, we need more people like you me who are willing show ours skills on how to produce basic machines cable of producing power of kinds, we can not rely on the technology alone, great videos mate 😀

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: 100% agree with you. mate
